description: The provided image displays a publisher interface badge labeled 'Check for updates,' featuring a circular icon with a red bookmark symbol and accompanying text. It contains no scientific data, micrographs, spectra, or plots relevant to materials science. Consequently, no material system, sample designation, processing condition, phase, or property metric can be visually extracted from the image itself. However, the accompanying textual context indicates that the associated paper investigates a Fe-31Mn-9Al-1C-0.45Nb-0.45Mo-0.45W austenitic low-density steel subjected to hot-rolling reductions of 45%, 68%, and 82%. The study characterizes microstructural evolution via SEM, EBSD, and TEM, and evaluates mechanical properties through tensile and Charpy impact testing. Because the visual content is purely a navigational user-interface element rather than an experimental figure, no trends, comparative relationships, peak positions, or morphological features can be observed or reported from the image pixels. The figure-level scientific conclusion must therefore rely entirely on the provided text extraction summary rather than direct visual evidence.

This figure presents a comprehensive suite of EBSD maps characterizing the microstructural evolution of a Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel subjected to increasing hot-rolling reductions: HR45 (45%), HR68 (68%), and HR82 (82%). Subfigures (a–c) are band contrast maps showing equiaxed face-centered cubic (FCC) austenite grains with a high density of annealing twins. Subfigures (d–f) are phase maps overlaid by grain boundary maps, where the FCC phase is colored blue, black lines denote high-angle grain boundaries with misorientation θ > 15°, and red lines mark Σ3 (60°〈111〉) twin boundaries; these reveal progressive grain refinement from a mean size of ~45 μm in HR45 to ~19 μm in HR82, alongside a decrease in the Σ3 twin boundary fraction from 67% to 52%. Subfigures (g–i) are kernel average misorientation (KAM) maps with a scale from 0° to 5°, showing increasingly pronounced green/yellow regions of high orientation gradient and local strain concentration with higher rolling reduction, indicating greater geometrically necessary dislocation accumulation. Collectively, the maps demonstrate that increasing hot-rolling reduction enhances dynamic recrystallization to refine grains and raises dislocation density, which are central to the observed strength enhancement.

This figure presents SEM backscattered-electron (BSE) images revealing the distribution of second-phase particles in a Fe-31Mn-9Al-1C-0.45Nb-0.45Mo-0.45W austenitic low-density steel subjected to different hot-rolling reductions. Panels (a–c) display micron-sized particles at lower magnification (scale bar 20 μm), while panels (d–f) show submicron-sized particles at higher magnification (scale bar 300 nm). The sample correspondence is (a, d) HR45 (45% reduction), (b, e) HR68 (68% reduction), and (c, f) HR82 (82% reduction). In (a–c), bright polygonal micron-sized particles are aligned along the rolling direction, primarily at austenite grain boundaries and in grain interiors. In (d–f), submicron-sized spherical particles are dispersed within the matrix. The associated text identifies these as (Nb,Mo,W)C complex carbide particles. Comparison across the three conditions indicates that increasing hot-rolling reduction refines the austenite grain size while retaining these carbide particles, which serve as preferential nucleation sites for microcracks yet contribute to precipitation strengthening and particle-stimulated nucleation of dynamic recrystallization.

Figure 3 presents integrated TEM characterization of a submicron-sized precipitate in the HR68 sample of a Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel. Panel (a) is a bright-field TEM image showing a roughly spherical particle with a red circle highlighting its central region. Panels (b)–(h) display corresponding EDS elemental maps: Fe, Mn, and Al maps exhibit pronounced depletion within the particle (dark contrast), whereas the Nb map (f) shows strong enrichment coinciding with the particle. The C map (e) appears visually uniform, and Mo (g) and W (h) maps show scattered bright spots. Panel (i) gives the [110] zone-axis SAED pattern with indexed FCC reflections ((002), (111), (1̄11)). Together with caption-reported EDS results, the data identify the precipitate as a face-centered cubic (Nb,Mo,W)C complex carbide with a lattice parameter of 4.36 Å, distinct from the austenite matrix and supporting its role in precipitation strengthening.

This figure presents TEM characterization of κ-carbide precipitation in a Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel subjected to different hot-rolling reductions. Panels (a–c) show selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns taken from the austenite matrix of the HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples along the [1 0 0] zone axis, respectively, where faint (0 0 1) and (0 1 0) superlattice reflections (marked with circles) indicate the presence of ordered κ-carbides. Panels (d–f) are the corresponding dark-field (DF) images using the (1 0 0) superlattice reflection, revealing homogeneous distributions of nano-sized precipitates within the austenite grains. The diffraction spots and DF images confirm the existence of (Fe,Mn)₃AlC-type κ-carbides with an L′1₂ structure formed during water quenching. According to the caption, the mean sizes and volume fractions of these κ-carbides remain statistically similar across all three samples (approximately 0.8 nm and 3–4%, respectively), suggesting that precipitation strengthening from κ-carbides contributes comparably to the yield strength of HR45, HR68, and HR82. Consequently, the observed increment in yield strength with increasing hot-rolling reduction is attributed primarily to grain refinement and dislocation strengthening rather than to variation in κ-carbide precipitation.

Figure 5 presents the mechanical properties of a Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel at hot-rolling reductions of 45%, 68%, and 82%. Subfigure (a) shows the room-temperature engineering stress-strain curves for samples HR45 (black), HR68 (red), and HR82 (blue). Visual inspection and caption reports indicate that increasing rolling reduction progressively raises both yield strength and ultimate tensile strength, with HR82 reaching approximately 976 MPa compared to 861 MPa for HR45, while total elongation decreases from about 60% to 50%. Subfigure (b) plots Charpy V-notch impact energy as a function of rolling reduction, revealing a downward trend from 133 ± 2.5 J/m² at 45% to 105 ± 1.2 J/m² at 82%. These data collectively support that higher hot-rolling reduction refines grain size and increases dislocation density, thereby strengthening the steel while maintaining excellent ductility and impact toughness above 100 J/m² across all conditions.

Figure 6 presents a set of SEM fractographs showing the tensile fracture surfaces of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel samples subjected to different hot-rolling reductions: (a) HR45 (45% reduction), (b) HR68 (68% reduction), and (c) HR82 (82% reduction). All three samples exhibit typical ductile fracture morphology characterized by abundant ductile dimples (circled) and microvoids (indicated by arrows) across the fracture surfaces. The microvoids are associated with debonding of intragranular (Nb,Mo,W)C carbide particles, which partially alleviates strain/stress concentration at intergranular carbide interfaces. With increasing rolling reduction, the ductile fracture character is retained, indicating that toughness is barely compromised despite progressive grain refinement with increased rolling reduction. Notably, the dimple size in HR45 is visibly larger than in HR68 and HR82, which the caption attributes to the larger mean grain size of the lower-reduction sample. These observations support the conclusion that the investigated steel maintains excellent toughness across the examined processing window, consistent with Charpy impact test results, and that intergranular carbide particles do not exert a detrimental effect on fracture resistance.

This figure presents a dual-axis scatter plot comparing the mechanical property balance of an Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel and various reference alloys. The horizontal axis shows yield strength (R_p0.2) ranging from approximately 300 to 1000 MPa. The left vertical axis (blue) reports Charpy impact energy in J/cm², while the right vertical axis (red) reports total elongation in %. Green stars, positioned at approximately 480 MPa / 130 J/cm², 580 MPa / 115 J/cm², and 650 MPa / 105 J/cm², represent the present steel after hot-rolling reductions of 45%, 68%, and 82%, respectively, showing the progressive increase in yield strength with rolling reduction. Blue symbols (solid and open circles, triangles, squares, and inverted triangles) denote Charpy impact energy data for comparison materials, and red symbols denote their total elongations. The plot reveals a general strength–ductility/toughness trade-off among reference alloys, including aging-hardened Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys with high yield strength but low elongation (~10–20%) and toughness, and solution-treated austenitic stainless steels or Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys with lower strength but higher ductility. The present steel maintains superior elongation (~50–60%) and impact energy (~100–130 J/cm²) even at elevated yield strengths, supporting the conclusion that increasing hot-rolling reduction achieves an exceptional strength–ductility–toughness synergy primarily through grain refinement and dislocation strengthening.

Figure 8 presents two quantitative line-graph panels that correlate microstructural parameters with hot-rolling reduction in a Fe-31Mn-9Al-1C-0.45Nb-0.45Mo-0.45W (wt%) austenitic low-density steel. Panel (a) plots the mean grain size excluding twins, the effective grain size including twin boundaries, and the dislocation density versus rolling reduction (45%, 68%, and 82%). The grain size excluding twins decreases from approximately 45 μm to 19 μm, the effective grain size including twins drops from roughly 22 μm to 7 μm, and the geometrically necessary dislocation density rises from about 2.7×10¹³ m⁻² to 9.9×10¹³ m⁻² as reduction increases. Panel (b) displays the volume fractions and mean particle sizes of κ-carbides and (Nb,Mo,W)C precipitates. The κ-carbide volume fraction remains in the range of ~3.3–3.9% with a mean size near 0.8 nm, while the (Nb,Mo,W)C volume fraction stays around 0.2% with a mean size of ~260 nm, both nearly unchanged with increasing reduction. Collectively, the figure demonstrates that higher hot-rolling reduction refines austenite grains and raises dislocation density without appreciably altering precipitate characteristics, supporting the core conclusion that the observed strength increment stems primarily from grain-boundary and dislocation strengthening rather than from precipitate strengthening.

Figure 9 presents quantitative strengthening mechanism analyses for a Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel processed at hot-rolling reductions of 45 % (HR45), 68 % (HR68), and 82 % (HR82). Panel (a) plots the true flow stress at yielding σ_F0—defined as σ_y minus precipitate strengthening σ_p and dislocation strengthening σ_ds—versus the inverse square root of the mean effective grain size d_e^(-1/2). The red Hall-Petch fitting line gives σ_F0 = 203.1 + 733.4 d_e^(-1/2) (MPa), yielding a friction stress of 203.1 MPa and a Hall-Petch coefficient of 733.4 MPa·μm^(1/2). The three visible data points correspond to effective grain sizes of 21.82 μm (359.5 MPa), 11.55 μm (420.0 MPa), and 7.02 μm (479.3 MPa). Panel (b) is a stacked bar chart illustrating the calculated contributions of σ_F (friction stress), σ_p-shearing, σ_p-Orowan, σ_ds (dislocation strengthening), and σ_gb (grain boundary strengthening) to the yield strength for each rolling state, with white triangles marking experimentally measured values (σ_exp). The figure shows that grain boundary strengthening is the predominant contributor and increases markedly with rolling reduction, while dislocation strengthening also rises; together they account for the yield strength increment. Caption states that the calculated results show good consistency with experimental data, validating the strengthening analysis.

Figure 10 displays the true stress (σ_t)-strain (ε_t) curves (solid lines) superimposed over the strain-hardening rate (dσ/dε)-true strain curves (dashed lines) for Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel samples subjected to hot-rolling reductions of 45% (HR45, black/grey), 68% (HR68, red), and 82% (HR82, blue). The solid lines show flow stress, while the dashed lines reveal a three-staged strain-hardening evolution for all samples: an initial drop after yielding (Stage I), a gradual rise to a peak (Stage II), and a continuous decrease until fracture (Stage III). The arrows mark interrupted true strains (ε_c ≈ 0.1, 0.2, and 0.4) where tensile tests were stopped, as stated in the caption. Visually, the strain-hardening rate remains at a high level near 2000 MPa across a broad plastic strain window, and the curves shift upward with increasing rolling reduction. The accompanying text reports that this distinct three-stage response, unlike the continuously decreasing trend in conventional high-SFE fcc metals, sustains high work-hardening capacity and thereby contributes to the superior strength-ductility combination.

This figure presents nine TEM bright-field (BF) micrographs taken along the <1 1 0> zone axis of the austenite matrix in a Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel, documenting the strain-dependent evolution of deformation substructures in samples subjected to 45% (HR45; a–c), 68% (HR68; d–f), and 82% (HR82; g–i) hot-rolling reduction after interrupted tensile tests at engineering strains (εe) of 0.1, 0.2, and 0.4. At εe = 0.1 (a, d, g), the micrographs reveal homogeneous arrays of planar {1 1 1} slip traces (marked by dashed lines) and Taylor lattices arising from intersecting non-coplanar slip systems. With increasing strain to 0.2 (b, e, h), slip bands become denser and more closely spaced, while microbands begin to appear in the HR68 and HR82 samples. At εe = 0.4 (c, f, i), microbands with ill-defined boundaries (arrows) and distinct boundaries (solid lines) are fully developed. The images collectively demonstrate that higher rolling reductions accelerate substructural evolution: finer initial grains lead to faster refinement of slip bands and earlier formation of microbands compared with the coarser-grained HR45 sample, supporting a transition from stage-II strain hardening dominated by slip-band refinement to stage-III hardening governed by microband development.

This line chart illustrates the evolution of slip band spacing as a function of engineering strain for a Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel processed at three hot-rolling reductions: HR45 (45%), HR68 (68%), and HR82 (82%). The x-axis covers engineering strain up to 0.4, while the y-axis reports slip band spacing in nanometers. At a strain of 0.1, measured spacings are approximately 296 nm for HR45, 155 nm for HR68, and 68 nm for HR82, each with visible error bars. With increasing strain, all three curves exhibit a monotonic decrease, with HR45 showing the steepest drop and HR82 the most rapid saturation. By a strain of 0.4, the values converge to roughly 50 nm, which corresponds to the saturation limit of slip band refinement. The figure supports the conclusion that larger rolling reductions produce finer initial grains, accelerating slip band refinement through higher back stresses on dislocation sources and enhancing stage-II strain hardening via increased long-range stress. However, the earlier saturation at ~50 nm in finer-grained samples promotes premature microband formation, shortening stage-II duration and accounting for the slight ductility reduction observed at higher reductions.
